task.title: Monitor CPU Utilization
task.description: Use existing system tools to retrieve current CPU usage metrics and identify any anomalies or performance deviations.

solution.approach: Analyze the system to determine available tools for retrieving CPU utilization metrics. Identify whether standard command-line utilities such as 'top', 'htop', 'mpstat', or 'vmstat' are present and suitable for capturing current CPU usage without requiring custom script development.
solution.algorithm: Check availability of common CLI monitoring tools via 'which' or direct execution; evaluate output format and real-time capability.

solution.approach: Design a lightweight monitoring strategy that leverages existing system tools to fetch CPU utilization data. Prefer non-intrusive, short-lived commands that provide immediate feedback. Select 'mpstat' if available due to its precise per-CPU statistics; otherwise fall back to 'top' in batch mode or parse '/proc/stat'. Avoid writing new Python scripts unless absolutely necessary.
solution.algorithm: Use conditional logic: attempt 'mpstat -P ALL 1 1', then 'top -bn1 | grep "Cpu(s)"', finally parse '/proc/stat' as fallback.

solution.approach: Execute the selected CLI command(s) to retrieve current CPU utilization metrics. Capture stdout directly without redirection to files. Use shell command execution with proper error handling and timeout safeguards.

Validate retrieved CPU metrics by checking for anomalies such as consistently high idle time (indicating underutilization), zero usage across all cores, or sudden spikes inconsistent with recent activity. Compare results against typical baseline expectations and flag deviations exceeding thresholds (e.g., >90% sustained usage or <5% on all CPUs).
solution.algorithm: Parse command output to extract %idle or %user/%system values. Apply threshold-based anomaly detection logic.

solution.approach: Analyze the system to determine available tools for retrieving CPU utilization metrics. Identify whether standard command-line utilities such as 'top', 'htop', 'mpstat', or 'vmstat' are present and suitable for capturing current CPU usage without requiring custom script development.
solution.algorithm: Check availability of common CLI monitoring tools via 'which' or direct execution; evaluate output format and real-time capability.

task.title: Monitor CPU Utilization

output.error: In .: Bash Validation Error: Command '['bash', '-n', '-c', 'mpstat -P ALL 1 1 || top -bn1 | grep "Cpu(s)" || cat /proc/stat']' timed out after 5 seconds

input.approach: Design a lightweight monitoring strategy that leverages existing system tools to fetch CPU utilization data. Prefer non-intrusive, short-lived commands that provide immediate feedback. Select 'mpstat' if available due to its precise per-CPU statistics; otherwise fall back to 'top' in batch mode or parse '/proc/stat'. Avoid writing new Python scripts unless absolutely necessary.

output.reflection: The diagnostic command chain timed out after 5 seconds, indicating a potential issue with one of the fallback tools or system responsiveness. The command attempted to use 'mpstat -P ALL 1 1' first (for per-CPU stats), then fell back to 'top -bn1 | grep Cpu(s)', and finally to parsing '/proc/stat'. Timeout suggests either mpstat is unresponsive, top hangs in batch mode under load, or /proc/stat access is blocked/slow. No output was captured due to timeout.

solution.approach: Execute the selected CLI command(s) to retrieve current CPU utilization metrics. Capture stdout directly without redirection to files. Use shell command execution with proper error handling and timeout safeguards.

task.title: Monitor CPU Utilization

output.error: In ./cpu_metrics.sh: Bash Validation Error: Command '['bash', '-n', '-c', 'timeout 5s mpstat -P ALL 1 1 2>&1 || timeout 5s top -bn1 2>&1 || cat /proc/stat 2>&1']' timed out after 5 seconds

input.approach: Execute the selected CLI command(s) to retrieve current CPU utilization metrics. Capture stdout directly without redirection to files. Use shell command execution with proper error handling and timeout safeguards.

output.reflection: The command execution timed out after 5 seconds, indicating a potential issue with the availability or responsiveness of system monitoring tools (mpstat, top). No CPU utilization data was captured. The fallback to /proc/stat also failed within the timeout window, suggesting either high system load, tool unavailability, or permission restrictions. This failure prevents proper diagnostic assessment of current CPU metrics.

solution.approach: Validate retrieved CPU metrics by checking for anomalies such as consistently high idle time (indicating underutilization), zero usage across all cores, or sudden spikes inconsistent with recent activity. Compare results against typical baseline expectations and flag deviations exceeding thresholds (e.g., >90% sustained usage or <5% on all CPUs).
solution.algorithm: Parse command output to extract %idle or %user/%system values. Apply threshold-based anomaly detection logic.

Identify whether standard command-line utilities such as 'top', 'htop', 'mpstat', or 'vmstat' are present and suitable for capturing current CPU usage without requiring custom script development.

output.reflection: The diagnostic task failed due to a fundamental system-level error: '/bin/bash' is not found or inaccessible. This indicates that the shell environment is either corrupted, improperly configured, or the WSL subsystem is in an unstable state. Since no command-line utilities can be executed without a working shell, it is impossible to assess the presence of CPU monitoring tools such as 'top', 'htop', 'mpstat', or 'vmstat'. The root cause appears to be a failure in process execution within the WSL relay layer, which prevents any shell-based diagnostics from proceeding.
